texto: Jews have been an ever-dying people that never died. They have experienced a continuous resurrection, like the dry bones that Ezekiel saw in the valley. This has become the sine qua non of every Jew. It is the mystery of the hidden miracle of survival in the face of overwhelming destruction. Our refusal to surrender has turned our story into one long, unending Purim tale.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Nathan Lopes Cardozo: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “Jews have been an ever-dying people that never died. They have experienced a continuous resurrect…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, miracles, purim pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e purim.

texto: Even if dogma has a purpose, it can never function as a substitute for faith, only as a dry aspect of it.
explicacao: Citação de Nathan Lopes Cardozo sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. “Even if dogma has a purpose, it can never function as a substitute for faith, only as a dry aspec…” articula motivacao, faith, inspiracional em registro de motivacao, com relevância para contextos como inspiracao e inspiracional.

texto: [I]t is no doubt true that our image of what a messiah might look like may keep us from recognizing the real thing when it stands before us. Could it be that we have embellished the long-awaited event with so many aggadic flourishes that we can no longer recognize the reality when it happens? Could our overly literal reading of our sages’ poetic descriptions have led us to overlook completely the miracle as it happened? One of the dangers of taking the statements and speculations of our sages as literal truth—when they were not meant as such—is the distortion of our expectations.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Nathan Lopes Cardozo: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “[I]t is no doubt true that our image of what a messiah might look like may keep us from recognizi…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, aggadah, messiah pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de messiah e messianic-age.

frase_original: [I]t is no doubt true that our image of what a messiah might look like may keep us from recognizing the real thing when it stands before us. Could it be that we have embellished the long-awaited event with so many aggadic flourishes that we can no longer recognize the reality when it happens? Could our overly literal reading of our sages’ poetic descriptions have led us to overlook completely the miracle as it happened? One of the dangers of taking the statements and speculations of our sages as literal truth—when they were not meant as such—is the distortion of our expectations.
